entry.message: Sandbox evaluated community message from sandbox.user.

input.message: Hi @tomas.ruiz_OBIC Can i get the war room link for today?

decision.reason: User is requesting the War Room link, which is clearly supported by project memory. The link is configured and should be provided.
decision.reply: Here is the War Room link: https://scale.zoom.us/j/91510346485?pwd=IEPPxvhcHt1W25AXq01eMC3Ynn5SsO.1#success. Once you enter, join the breakout room called Stargazer - Team.
guidelineSnippets:
As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOCî‚‚
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
â—ڈ Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonت¼t work.
â—ڈ Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
â—ڈ Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
â—ڈ Click on View API Key
â—ڈ Open Cursor Settings â†’ Models
â—ڈ Disable all models, and add:
â—‹ claude-qwen3î‚ˆ5î‚ˆ27b-scale
â—‹ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
â—ڈ Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
â—ڈ Click "Verify".
â—ڈ Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
â—ڈ Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
â—ڈ Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
â—ڈ Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.
Project Structure (return to TOCî‚‚

â—ڈ Think ahead. Before writing, plan how the gold solution and tests will look. If you
cannot picture them, the prompt is not ready.
2.2 Rate the difficulty of your issue (return to TOCî‚‚
Rate your issue by counting how many of the following complexity signals apply:
1. Spread: The fix changes 3 or more files.
2. Cross-module interaction: The fix affects more than one part of the project.
3. Architectural awareness: To fix it, you need to understand how the project is
organized.
4. Non-trivial logic: The fix needs decision-making in the code, not just renaming,
moving, or deleting something.
5. Implicit edge cases: The solution should also handle special cases or possible
errors.
6. Regression risk: A bad fix could accidentally break something else.
Difficulty Signals that apply
Easy 1 - 2
Medium 3 - 4
Hard 5î‚‌
2.3 Create the Issue (return to TOCî‚‚
Location: App/
â—ڈ For all issue types except Bug Injection, the files inside app/ must remain identical
to the original repo.
â—ڈ For Bug Injection, introduce the bug directly inside the app/ folder so that this
modified version represents the faulty instance state that the solution will fix.
2.3.1 Generate Base-to-Instance Patch
You must generate an additional patch called basetoinstance.patch. If the issue type is
Bug Injection, after introducing the bug, you need to capture the exact diff between the
working base state and the broken instance state

Stargazer Axiom Guidelines
Welcome! This document outlines the process and structure for creating issues for the
Stargazer Axiom project, including code base preparation, issue creation, solution
development, test writing, and setting up Docker files and execution scripts.

Changelog
Date Updates
June 10 â†’ NEWî‚’ Step 0 - Project Onboarding
May 28 â†’ model_trace.patch is now required for model failure trace
Project Overview
The purpose of this project is to create a database of challenging issue requests for a
repository. This database will be used to benchmark and improve the ability of coding
agents to implement changes in a codebase in response to user prompts.
To achieve this, you will build a standard project setup around an assigned repository. You
will then create a challenging prompt requesting changes to the codebase. You will check
that the model fails to respond adequately to your prompt, write your own solution to the
issue, create unit tests to verify your solution, and compose a rubric that can be used to
grade how successful the agent was in responding to your issue. When you are finished,
you will prepare the required patches, scripts, and Docker files so the whole workflow can
be reproduced and validated later.
